So when you do that, as soon as they step into your arena, it's my belief, I learned through life skills and some behavioral reconditioning. It was mentioned that when a baby's born, right, and that two to four hour window where the mother is holding the baby, there's a strong emotional connection that happens that's integral to the subconscious development of that child. I believe the same thing applies to salespeople, right? If you don't expose your salespeople to the nooks and crannies of your business, what it does, who it impacts, why it matters to the leaders, and how it happens, and you're letting your salesperson go off into the wilderness like a motherless baby, and good luck with that, because that's where problems happen, right? So the moment that your salesperson feels like they're a salesperson and they just have a sales job is the moment you start to lose your salespeople, and a misaligned, or out of alignment salesperson, that's how sales departments crumble from the inside out. So I was actually at six pack shortcuts yesterday and the day before, and it was really cool for me because it's been two years since I've been there, and it goes to show how important this is, because if you listen to the floor over at, and I'm not comparing, it's a totally different product, totally different customer, but just how important it is to take control as a leader, as a business owner of the behavioral patterns and just the behaviors of your salespeople, how it affects the culture, it's a habitual environment. So I do not refer to, you know, we did zero to a million in, I think it was nine months over at Lady Boss, and we don't call our sales team a sales department, because when you use the terminology sales department, it's almost separating the sales department from the mission. It's kind of like the sales people are on their own island, right? And what happens is too, is when you condition your salespeople with the mindset, and it's true, they are the heartbeat of the company, but when you condition them as the hierarchy of the company, then they start to take on this personality trait that they're owed something. Has anyone here ever had salespeople where they felt like they were owed something, or I wanna be paid more, or I deserve more? Anyone ever have that? Yeah, right? As opposed to rather being earned. So XYZ phone team establishes a team connection to the mission of your business, and it brands the department. Music can be... Yeah. Yeah. So music is a huge... So if you've got a big sales team, or whether you've got a remote sales team, especially you've got a remote sales team, one of the things that I would do is, if you have a remote sales team, keep a computer up, keep everybody on Zoom so they're held accountable, and then have music playing from a universal player so everybody can hear the same thing. And in our... So in our... We have everybody in-house. We jack the music. Six-pack shortcuts. It's like you're going to a freaking concert. It's... But it works, right? When there's energy, when there's... Boom, boom, boom, boom, right? Bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, bang, right? So like, make sure you have good music for your people, and again, hydrate it on the hour. All right, guys, coach your people to unconscious competence. If you don't know what this is, I'm going to teach you what it is, because it's important that you teach your people to know what it is. So unconscious... There's four levels of competence, right? And the goal for any salesperson is to get so good that they can read the script, they can handle objections, they can handle any scenario that they face, whether it's a customer complaint... Anything is unconscious competence where they can do it with their eyes closed. So unconscious incompetence is basically when you suck and you don't know that you suck, right? Conscious incompetence is where you wake up and you're like, oh, man, I suck. I can do sign, make some changes. Conscious competence is where you get good, but you have to put all your focus... So you're tying your shoe and you're like, God, I got it, someone comes over, shut up, I can't right now, I got to focus, right? If they interrupt you, then you'll screw up. But unconscious competence is where you're so good, you can just do it with your eyes closed, right? That's why I loved about theater when memorizing scripts. Who is it that complains with the king that I pursue and stern and love him not? By holy Paul, they love his grace, but lightly the Phil's eyes with the centrist rumors. Just memorizing the script, like Shakespeare, like just cold, right? You want to get your people to that point. It usually takes about 60 to 90 days to do that. So what will help you guys get your salespeople with your scripts, with your product, to understand your company model, how you operate, doesn't matter, you can take this template and you can start doing this today, tomorrow. The perfect one-on-one sales coaching template. Every single day, all my salespeople get a coaching session. I am a huge believer in coaching. I know some companies do like 90-day onboarding. No, man. Sales is an everyday thing, right? It takes a different mindset, it's a different breed of personality. You've got to be coaching your people. So what I do is I go over one victory. Super simple, right? One victory and I celebrate with my person. And make sure that when you're coaching them that you're walking with them, not sitting down. Because if you give them good stuff and you're up, you're moving around, they're going to memorize it better. What I've learned after doing this for the amount of years I've done it. One challenge, you know, hey Jim, what's one challenge that you had today? And hear them out, listen, and then resolve it. And then train their bridging the gap muscle. So the thing about sales is I like to look at sales a lot like bodybuilding, right? You know, like Branch Warren, he's got amazing quads, but like he's got a weird upper body, you know what I mean? Doreen Yeats, same thing, which is massive, but like, not symmetrical. Then you have someone like Arnold Schwarzenegger, just like perfect physique, right? It's kind of the same thing with sales, right? You're going to have someone that has a really strong intro and tonality, but when they get to the close, they start to like shy away, right? So it's important as you go through the one-on-one coaching, you want to find out what muscle in their sales game is weak and just do more reps, do more sets, right? And train that muscle because Brian Tracy says it's usually one thing out of the seven areas where they don't even know that they're doing it wrong, where if you fix it, it'll just, right? So for me, it was really listening. So I noticed that like at Lady Boss, especially from my male salespeople, is that go figure a male, ladies are like, yeah, tell them, Nick, where the guy listens to the girl, right? You don't listen to me ever, right? So, but I noticed that's like a big challenge. So like, you know, pulling, if you don't have a recording, whether it's 5'9", or Ring Central, or Google Voice, recording your sales conversations with your salespeople, if you are doing it over the phone, or if it's in person, making sure that you're like shadowing your people, just checking in and listening. So you can find those weak areas. A lot of times, it's people just don't really listen. Like they'll give them like a golden nugget and then they don't act on it. So that's what bridging the gap muscle is. It just gives you that one-on-one time to really help them improve that area. And then what I do is that I do an objection handle run-through. So like for us at Lady Boss, our number, like our top four or five objections is, I can't afford it. I'm sure you guys have all heard that one. I can't afford it. Now is not the time, or I just want to try it out. That's really the two that we deal with the most. But whatever it is, I'll run through it real quick with them. So like if you and I are having a one-on-one coaching, we're going to focus, we're just going to boom, boom, boom, boom. It's like immersion-based sales training. So I'll be like, hey man, I just can't afford it. And then you go, and you respond to me. Or hey man, I need to think about it, and then you go. And if they don't do it right, I haven't started over again. We just keep doing that and doing that and doing that and doing that and doing that and doing that every single day until it's like, they just pull it out. Hey man, I didn't think about it. Yeah Jim, I totally hear you, but the fact of the matter is, whether you do this today or tomorrow or next month, is it going to change the fact that you want this and need this? Well let's just get you what you want and what you need, and let's get you started today. Okay, be a surmaster card. Right? It's going to cost you under bucks, I'm just kidding. Right? But that's how we do really fast training, because that's what gets your person unconscious competence. So homework assignment for you guys is to list out your top five objections, write out your top three ways to handle it, and then when you do the one-on-one coaching, first off, you want to get it to your salespeople and say, hey study this, I want you to memorize this. Spend 15, 30 minutes a night, just read over these objections. And when you do your one-on-one coaching, hey man, we're going to do an objection run-through, right? So remember those five objections I gave you with the three responses? Okay, cool. So what I'm going to do is I'm going to tell you that I can't do this and I got to think about it, I got to da-da-da, and I got to da-da-da, and da-da-da, and then you're going to give me the objection right away, and if you mess up, you're going to start over. For any fitness platform, it gets your closes in 10 minutes. We'll have to take a picture of this. I believe that there's seven sales that you have to make before you actually make a sale. What are you trying to do? Right? Just keep them on the phone. Sale number two is that your advice should outweigh the price of the product. Momentous baby steps. Meet them where they are. So in our script, we have women that signed up for a free trial, and we get one of two women. They either started or they didn't, right? And whether that's that or the six-week challenge, maybe you check up on them and you want to upsell them, I don't know. But I believe that in the beginning of the conversation, if you give them some type of baby steps that meet somewhere they are, they'll be like, wow, that's really cool. Because what it gives them a sense of is like, wow, when I get off this phone, I know exactly this next thing to do. And instead of like thinking that you're a sales person trying to close them, they're going to be like, wow, this person is really leading me right now and they're going to help me get to where I want to be. That's the second sale. Discovery, stimulate massive urgency. Being the loving doctor, loading your ammunition deck, future-paced pain. So Tony Robbins, when he used to help people quit smoking, he used to have them go buy a carton of cigarettes, bring them up to a hotel room, lock the hotel room, put a bunch of cigarettes between their fingers, light it up, smoke the whole thing until the carton was done, right? Why did he do that? Well, the reason why he did that is because he was helping them associate a massive amount of pain to the behavior of smoking cigarettes. So the thing I love about fitness is that this part's really easy, right? It's just digging deep on what their pain is, right? Hey, Jim, has the belly found the front, the side, the back, or all three? Okay, got it. Got it. Is it like one of three inches or is it like five plus? Okay, cool. Can you pinch her or can you grab it? Right. Jim, now be honest with me, man, because I want to help you here. When you put your t-shirt on, can you actually see the body fat? Right? So stimulating massive urgency. I'm not doing it to be an A-piece. I'm doing it because this guy has 100-plus pounds to lose. And if I don't help him wake up to the fact that he's been treating himself like crap and to get it hurt a little bit, then he's not going to move away from that. Right? So I need to wake him up a little bit. And I do that by just digging deep, right, the NLP language. Sale number four, give them a phone call that they never had, but they will always remember. Find the hot button. So Brian Tracy, the psychology of selling, he talks about this real estate agent and the wife and the husband, they walk into a house and the real estate agent was a great closer because he was looking out for the red hot button. So the red hot button was this red oak cherry tree in the back. And the husband's like, the floors are crappy. He's like, the real estate agent was like, yeah, because of the wife said, oh my God, honey, look at that red oak cherry tree. And he knew right away that's the red hot button. He's like, yeah, the floors do need work. But if you take a look out back right through that kitchen window, you can see the red oak cherry tree. Husband walks upstairs. The bedrooms are small. Yeah, yeah, totally. They're not, you know, like what the rest of the town has. But if you look out the window, you can see the beautiful oak cherry tree. And the wife's eyes are like, oh my God, they end up buying the house. What's their red hot button? Why does this matter to them? Why is it important that they lose 50 pounds? Right? What's going to be their fight when they feel like quitting and resorting back to the Ben and Jerry's ice cream at midnight, seven days a week? What's their fight? Right? And then once you find out what's really important to them, press it over and over and over and over and over again until you ask them to take action. Well, let's sing them a song. I think all you guys know it. I mean, if somebody wants to just kick it off, I'll drop a beat or something. No, I'm just kidding. Happy birthday to you. Happy birthday to you. Happy birthday dear Rob. Happy birthday to you. Happy birthday Rob. And anyone else that didn't stand up because they were too shy, because I know that there's probably somebody out there. Anyway, we're going to break until 3.15. Is that right? 3.15? I'm sure that there's snacks and coffee in the foyer and we'll reconvene then. Thanks guys.
